topic: The Iran War Powers fight: did Trump break the Constitution by keeping the war going without Congress — or is the House's rebuke a symbolic, unconstitutional intrusion on the commander-in-chief?
question: After the House passed a war powers resolution 215-208 directing the president to end hostilities with Iran, is the left correct that Trump is waging an unauthorized war past the 60-day War Powers clock — or is the right correct that the strikes fall within the president's Article II authority, the ceasefire has ended 'hostilities,' and the resolution is a symbolic, vetoable measure that intrudes on the commander-in-chief mid-negotiation?
summary:
On June 3, 2026, the Republican-led House passed a war powers resolution 215-208 — four Republicans (Thomas Massie, Brian Fitzpatrick, Tom Barrett, Warren Davidson) joining every Democrat — directing President Trump to remove U.S. armed forces from hostilities against Iran absent a declaration of war or specific authorization. It followed a May 19 Senate vote (50-47) that advanced a parallel resolution, S.J.Res.59, led by Sen. Tim Kaine, with four Republicans crossing over (Collins, Murkowski, Paul, Cassidy). Trump called the House action 'a meaningless vote' by '4 bad Republicans and all of the Dumocrats,' branded the four 'GRANDSTANDERS!', and signaled a veto. By June 8-9 it had hardened into a sustained constitutional argument about who controls the use of force.

The checkable core is genuinely two-sided. The factual spine of the critics' case is strong: the war began Feb. 28, 2026, with U.S.-Israeli strikes; Congress never authorized it; and under the 1973 War Powers Resolution the president must end unauthorized hostilities within 60 days (a window that closed in late April / early May). The administration declared military action 'terminated' on the strength of an April 8 ceasefire — but documented strikes have continued in the Gulf and Lebanon through at least June 7, undercutting the claim that 'hostilities' have ended. The right's spine is also partly real: the resolution is largely symbolic (the Senate has not given final passage, and Trump will veto with no veto-proof majority in sight), and the president's Article II / commander-in-chief authority to order limited strikes is a long-standing executive position — codified in a 1992 Office of Legal Counsel two-part test and invoked by presidents of both parties (Obama's 2011 Libya campaign, Trump's 2018 Syria strikes and 2020 Soleimani strike).

On the present-tense, checkable facts, the left's load-bearing claims — the 60-day clock was crossed, there was no congressional authorization, and hostilities are in fact ongoing — hold up better than the right's central factual predicate that the war is 'terminated.' But the headline charge that the strikes are flatly 'unconstitutional' is a genuinely contested legal question, not a settled fact: no court has ruled, the executive's Article II theory is decades old and bipartisan in practice, and Congress itself has never overridden a war-powers veto. The needle leans slightly left because the critics' factual predicates are stronger, but it stays near the middle: the deepest dispute is a real constitutional disagreement the meter cannot resolve, and the right's procedural and Article II points are not frivolous.
verdict: On the checkable facts, the critics edge ahead, but only slightly. Three of the left's load-bearing claims are well-supported and largely conceded: the Iran war began Feb. 28 with no congressional authorization, the War Powers Resolution's 60-day clock lapsed in late April / early May, and 'hostilities' have demonstrably continued despite the April 8 ceasefire (U.S.-Iran strikes traded in the Gulf, Israeli strikes on Beirut, and Iranian missiles on June 7). That makes the administration's central factual defense — that the war is 'terminated' so the clock doesn't bite — the weakest link in the chain. The right's better points are also real, which is why this isn't a blowout: the resolution is symbolic and vetoable, the Senate has not passed it, and the president's Article II authority for limited strikes rests on a decades-old executive legal theory used by Obama (Libya, 2011) as much as Trump. Where the left overreaches is in treating 'unconstitutional' as a settled fact: it is a contested legal question no court has decided, and presidents of both parties have defied or narrowed the same statute. The loudest part of this fight — whether the war is wise, whether regime change was justified, whether Congress should reclaim war powers — is a values and policy dispute the meter deliberately leaves unscored.
verificationNote: The two chamber votes were verified across cross-spectrum reporting: the House 215-208 vote (June 3) and the four crossover Republicans (Massie, Fitzpatrick, Barrett, Davidson) are confirmed by PolitiFact/PBS, TIME, MSNBC (ms.now), and Al Jazeera; the Senate 50-47 advance (May 19) and its four crossover Republicans (Collins, Murkowski, Paul, Cassidy) by CBS News and PolitiFact. The War Powers Resolution mechanics (48-hour report, 60-day clock, 30-day withdrawal, contested constitutionality) were taken from the National Constitution Center, Cornell LII's Wex, and the Wikipedia War Powers Resolution entry, which also documents the executive's 1992 OLC two-part test (used for Libya 2011, Syria 2018, Soleimani 2020). The conflict's Feb. 28, 2026 start date is confirmed by Britannica and Wikipedia; ongoing ceasefire violations through June 7 by an Al Jazeera timeline and the Wikipedia ceasefire entry. Trump's 'meaningless vote / 4 bad Republicans / GRANDSTANDERS' quote is confirmed verbatim across The Hill, The New Republic, and NPR's reporting. Trump's 2020 Article II veto language is quoted from the primary White House veto message (trumpwhitehouse.archives.gov). Congress.gov (S.J.Res.59), CNN, NPR, Washington Post, and TIME pages returned HTTP 403/451 and could not be fetched directly; their figures were corroborated through readable cross-spectrum outlets. Casualty and cost figures ($29B; 13 U.S. troop deaths; ~3,468 Iranian deaths) are party/agency-sourced and carried at medium/low confidence with caveats.

left.label: An unauthorized war past the War Powers deadline — Congress, not one man, decides war
left.framing: Congress alone holds the power to declare war (Article I). Trump launched a war on Iran on Feb. 28 with no congressional authorization, and the 1973 War Powers Resolution required him to end unauthorized hostilities within 60 days — a deadline that lapsed in late April. He dodged it by declaring the war 'terminated' under an April ceasefire that both sides keep violating, with U.S. and Iranian forces still trading fire. A bipartisan House majority has now formally directed him to stop. This is exactly the unchecked, unilateral war-making the Founders and the post-Vietnam Congress tried to prevent.
left.strongestPoint: The present-tense factual predicates are well-supported and cross-spectrum: the war began Feb. 28, 2026; Congress never authorized it; the 60-day War Powers clock closed in late April/early May; and 'hostilities' have demonstrably continued past the ceasefire (Al Jazeera's timeline and the ceasefire entry document repeated U.S.-Iran strikes and a June 7 Iranian missile barrage). Four Republicans in each chamber crossing party lines is strong corroboration that the procedural objection isn't merely partisan.
left.weakestPoint: Calling the strikes flatly 'unconstitutional' overstates a genuinely contested legal question: no court has ruled, the executive's Article II / OLC theory is decades old, and presidents of the left's own party (Obama in Libya, 2011) blew past the same 60-day clock with the same 'these aren't really hostilities' argument the left now condemns. The resolution is also largely symbolic — the Senate hasn't given final passage and a veto is certain — so the implication that it binds Trump now outruns the facts.

right.label: A symbolic, vetoable rebuke that intrudes on the commander-in-chief mid-negotiation
right.framing: The president's Article II commander-in-chief power lets him order limited strikes to protect national interests — a position every modern president has taken and the executive's own legal office has codified. The April ceasefire ended the war, so the War Powers '60-day' clock doesn't apply, and what remains is sporadic friction, not a 'war' in the constitutional sense. The House resolution is a largely symbolic, vetoable stunt by a handful of grandstanding Republicans and Democrats that would handcuff the president precisely while he is closing a peace deal. The Senate hasn't even passed it.
right.strongestPoint: Two factual/procedural claims are solid: the resolution is symbolic and vetoable (the Senate has not given final passage, and there is no veto-proof majority — no war-powers resolution has ever survived a presidential veto), and the president's Article II authority for limited strikes is a long-standing, bipartisan executive position grounded in the 1992 OLC two-part test and exercised by Obama (Libya), Trump (Syria, Soleimani), and others. The 'this isn't a full war' framing is not invented — it is the same legal theory Democratic administrations have used.
right.weakestPoint: The central factual predicate — that 'hostilities' have ended, so the War Powers clock doesn't apply — is contradicted by the record: U.S. and Iranian forces have traded strikes in the Gulf, the U.S. struck southern Iran in late May, and Iran fired missiles at Israel on June 7. Defending the strikes as plainly within Article II also treats a contested constitutional question as settled in the president's favor; the same executive who calls the War Powers Resolution unconstitutional cannot simultaneously claim its 60-day safe harbor was satisfied by a ceasefire that isn't holding.

facts.claim: On June 3, 2026, the House passed a war powers resolution 215-208 directing the president to end hostilities with Iran, with four Republicans joining all Democrats.
facts.evidence: PolitiFact/PBS: 'House Vote (June 3, 2026): Passed 215-208, with four Republicans joining Democrats: Thomas Massie (KY), Tom Barrett (MI), Warren Davidson (OH), and Brian Fitzpatrick (PA).' TIME and MSNBC report the same count, date, and four names.
facts.explanation: The count, date, and the four crossover Republicans are confirmed identically across left-leaning (MSNBC), center (PolitiFact/PBS), and international (Al Jazeera) outlets. This is the central event both sides are arguing about; it is not in dispute. Note one early search snippet listed the Senate's GOP crossovers instead — those four (Collins, Murkowski, Paul, Cassidy) belong to the May 19 Senate vote, a separate action.
facts.source: PolitiFact — The war powers resolution on the Iran war: What's next?

corroboration.source: TIME — House Votes to Restrain Trump's Iran War Powers in Bipartisan Rebuke
Al Jazeera — US House passes Iran war powers resolution in rebuke to Trump

facts.claim: The 2026 Iran war began on Feb. 28, 2026, with U.S. and Israeli strikes, and Congress never passed an authorization for the use of force.
facts.evidence: Britannica: the conflict began Feb. 28, 2026, when 'U.S. and Israeli forces began conducting joint strikes on Iran, numbering nearly 900 in just the first 12 hours.' PolitiFact and the constitutional analyses describe the action as undertaken without congressional authorization, which is the premise of the resolution itself.
facts.explanation: The start date is corroborated by Britannica and Wikipedia. That there was no AUMF or declaration of war is not contested by either side — the entire dispute is whether the president needed one, not whether he obtained one. This underpins the left's 60-day-clock argument.

facts.claim: The 1973 War Powers Resolution requires the president to terminate unauthorized hostilities within 60 days (plus a 30-day withdrawal window), a deadline that lapsed in late April / early May 2026.
facts.evidence: National Constitution Center: the Resolution requires a 48-hour report, a '60-Day Limit: Military action must end within 60 days unless Congress authorizes continuation,' and advance consultation. PolitiFact: 'The deadline arrived May 1, but Trump declared military action 'terminated' due to an alleged ceasefire.' Sixty days from Feb. 28 is late April.
facts.explanation: The statutory mechanics (50 U.S.C. Ch. 33) are well-established and cross-confirmed by the Constitution Center and Cornell LII. The factual question is application, not text: the clock exists, it lapsed, and the administration's answer is that 'hostilities' had ended — a claim the next fact tests.
facts.source: National Constitution Center — Does the War Powers Resolution debate take on new context in the Iran conflict?

corroboration.source: PolitiFact — The war powers resolution on the Iran war: What's next?

facts.claim: Despite an April 8 ceasefire that Trump cited to declare the war 'terminated,' U.S. and Iranian forces (and Israel) have continued to exchange strikes through at least June 7, 2026.
facts.evidence: Al Jazeera's timeline documents repeated incidents during the ceasefire (e.g., late-May U.S. strikes on southern Iran targeting 'missile sites and boats,' the IRGC downing a U.S. drone). Britannica: after an Israeli strike on southern Beirut on June 7, 'Iran launched waves of ballistic missiles toward Israel.' PolitiFact notes 'hostilities have continued periodically' with U.S. naval patrols in the strait.
facts.explanation: Graded supported at medium confidence: the individual incidents are reported by Al Jazeera (a party-adjacent regional outlet) and Britannica, and the overall pattern of a fraying ceasefire is corroborated by PolitiFact. The exact attribution of each strike is contested by the combatants, but the load-bearing point — that 'hostilities' did not cleanly end — is well-supported and directly undercuts the administration's 'terminated' defense.
facts.source: Al Jazeera — US, Iran have launched multiple attacks during ceasefire: A timeline

corroboration.source: PolitiFact — The war powers resolution on the Iran war: What's next?

facts.claim: The president's Article II commander-in-chief authority to order limited strikes is a long-standing executive legal position, codified in a 1992 OLC two-part test and invoked by presidents of both parties.
facts.evidence: Wikipedia (War Powers Resolution): the executive's 'two-part test' asks 'whether the President could reasonably determine that the action serves important national interests' and whether it would 'bring the Nation into a war'; it 'originated in a 1992 OLC memo and has most recently been used to justify the January 2020 strike that killed... Qassem Soleimani, the 2018 strikes against Syria, and the 2011 campaign of airstrikes against Libya.'
facts.explanation: This is the right's strongest factual point and it is solid: the Article II theory is not a Trump invention but a bipartisan executive practice. It does not prove the theory is correct — that is a contested constitutional question — but it establishes that limited strikes without prior authorization have a long, cross-party pedigree, which weakens the claim that this war is uniquely lawless.

corroboration.source: The White House (archived) — Presidential Veto Message for S.J. Res. 68 (2020)

facts.claim: The House resolution is largely symbolic: the Senate has not given final passage, Trump will veto, and no veto-proof majority exists — no war powers resolution has ever survived a presidential veto.
facts.evidence: PolitiFact: 'The Senate needs 60 votes for final passage... Even if both chambers pass identical language... Trump would likely veto, and overriding requires two-thirds majorities... No war powers resolution has ever survived a presidential veto.' MSNBC: 'a symbolic blow to Trump,' with no veto-proof majority and the Senate yet to pass a similar measure.
facts.explanation: Supported across center and left outlets. There is a genuine sub-dispute the right glosses: some Democrats argue a concurrent resolution can bind without the president's signature, but the operative reality is that this measure cannot force action over a veto as things stand. The right's 'symbolic' characterization is accurate; what it underplays is that symbolic rebukes still carry legal and political weight.
facts.source: PolitiFact — The war powers resolution on the Iran war: What's next?

corroboration.source: MSNBC (ms.now) — House adopts war powers resolution to rein in Trump on Iran

facts.claim: Trump dismissed the House vote as 'a meaningless vote' by '4 bad Republicans and all of the Dumocrats' and called the four crossover Republicans 'GRANDSTANDERS!'
facts.evidence: Per The Hill and The New Republic, Trump posted: 'Yesterday, in a meaningless vote, the House voted, 4 bad Republicans and all of the Dumocrats, to limit my War Powers, right in the middle of my final negotiations to end the War with the Islamic Republic of Iran,' and called the four 'GRANDSTANDERS!'
facts.explanation: The quote is confirmed verbatim across The Hill, The New Republic, and NPR's reporting of the post. It documents the right's framing (the rebuke handcuffs the president mid-negotiation) and is not in dispute.
facts.source: The Hill — Trump knocks Republicans who backed Iran war powers votes: 'Grandstanders!'

corroboration.source: The New Republic — Trump Flips Out Over Growing Republican Revolt on Iran

facts.claim: Whether the strikes are 'unconstitutional' is a settled question against the president.
facts.evidence: Cornell LII / Wikipedia: 'There is controversy over whether the War Powers Resolution's constraints on the president's authority as Commander-in-Chief are consistent with the Constitution,' and presidents report 'consistent with' rather than 'pursuant to' the Resolution precisely because they dispute its constitutionality. No court has resolved the question.
facts.explanation: Graded mixed: critics (e.g., Sen. Markey: 'illegal and unconstitutional') state as settled fact something that is genuinely contested. The Article I 'declare war' clause and the 60-day statute support the critics; the Article II commander-in-chief clause, decades of unbroken executive practice, and the absence of any court ruling support the president. The honest answer is that constitutionality is unresolved — which is why this is scored mixed, not supported, even though the left's narrower procedural claims hold.

corroboration.source: National Constitution Center — Does the War Powers Resolution debate take on new context in the Iran conflict?

facts.claim: By early June the Iran war had cost roughly $29 billion and killed 13 U.S. service members, with about 60% of Americans disapproving of Trump's handling.
facts.evidence: Breaking Defense: as of May 12 the cost 'to the US military was estimated at nearly US$29 billion' (excluding base damage). CNN documents the 13 U.S. troop deaths tied to the war. A PBS/NPR/Marist poll (May 2026) found about 33% approve and 60% disapprove of the war and Trump's handling.
facts.explanation: Graded mostly-supported at medium confidence: the $29B figure is a Pentagon estimate and rises if base damage and broader costs are included (Al Jazeera notes a range from ~$25B to over $1 trillion by some measures); the 13 U.S. deaths are confirmed but one outlet alleges the Pentagon trimmed wounded counts; the polling is a single firm's May survey. These figures contextualize the political pressure behind the vote but are not the load-bearing legal claims, so confidence is medium.
facts.source: Breaking Defense — Price tag for Iran war ticks up to $29B, not including base damage

corroboration.source: CNN — The lives behind the 13 US troop deaths tied to the Iran war

powerLens.thesis: War powers is the cleanest power-tracking issue in American politics: the party out of the White House discovers Article I and the sanctity of the 60-day clock, while the party in the White House discovers Article II and the limits of an 'unconstitutional' statute. The role-reversed mirror is exact and recent — President Obama's 2011 Libya campaign, where a Democratic White House blew through the same 60-day deadline using the same 'these aren't really hostilities' argument Trump uses now, while Republicans (Speaker Boehner) played the role Democrats play today.

precedent.case: Obama's 2011 Libya air campaign and the 60-day clock
precedent.what: Obama notified Congress of the Libya intervention on March 21, 2011; the 60-day War Powers deadline fell on May 20. Rather than seek authorization, the administration argued the law did not apply because U.S. operations were 'distinct from the kind of "hostilities" contemplated by the resolution's 60-day term' — leaning on a 1992-style OLC theory that NATO-led airstrikes without U.S. ground troops were not a 'war' requiring congressional approval. Speaker John Boehner sent Obama a letter warning he was out of time under the statute.
precedent.mirror: Then: a Democratic president redefined 'hostilities' to dodge the 60-day clock, and Republicans invoked the War Powers Resolution to object. Now: a Republican president redefines 'hostilities' (a ceasefire 'terminated' the war) to dodge the 60-day clock, and Democrats invoke the War Powers Resolution to object. The legal maneuver — narrow 'hostilities' so the clock never bites — is identical and bipartisan; only the party labels flipped.
precedent.source: ProPublica — What Exactly Is the War Powers Act and Is Obama Really Violating It?

corroboration.source: Wikipedia — War Powers Resolution (OLC two-part test; Libya 2011)

flips.then: In 2011, House Republicans under Speaker Boehner pressed Obama on the Libya 60-day deadline and warned he was violating the War Powers Resolution; in 2019-2020 most Republicans backed Trump's vetoes of war powers resolutions and defended robust executive war-making only when their own president was acting.
flips.now: In 2026, the Republican leadership (Speaker Johnson: it is 'a very dangerous prospect to take away from the administration and the commander-in-chief... the ability to negotiate') treats the same statutory clock as an intrusion on Article II, while branding the four Republicans who voted to enforce it 'grandstanders.'
flips.source: MSNBC (ms.now) — House adopts war powers resolution to rein in Trump on Iran

flips.then: In 2011, most congressional Democrats defended Obama's Libya campaign and accepted the administration's argument that limited airstrikes were not 'hostilities' triggering the 60-day clock — the very theory they now call lawless.
flips.now: In 2026, Democrats (led by Sen. Kaine, who in 2020 ran an almost identical Iran resolution Trump vetoed) insist the 60-day clock is binding and that redefining 'hostilities' to evade it is an unconstitutional power grab.
flips.source: ProPublica — What Exactly Is the War Powers Act and Is Obama Really Violating It?

powerLens.longRun: Strip out who holds the White House and the durable through-line is a 50-year, bipartisan executive landgrab: every president since 1973, of both parties, has treated the War Powers Resolution as advisory, narrowed 'hostilities' to fit the mission, and dared Congress to override a veto — which it never has. The opposition party always rediscovers Article I; the incumbent always rediscovers Article II. The prediction: the next Democratic president who orders strikes will invoke the same Article II theory Trump uses now, and today's Republican defenders of executive war-making will rediscover the 60-day clock the moment the commander-in-chief wears the other party's label.
powerLens.appliedToScore: Both flips are documented and neither side earns a reasoning-honesty bonus for its professed constitutional principle: each party has abandoned it in power. That symmetry is why the needle sits near the middle rather than swinging hard left. But the power test does not equalize today's checkable facts: whether the 60-day clock lapsed, whether Congress authorized the war, and whether 'hostilities' are in fact ongoing are present-tense factual questions, and on those the critics' predicates are better supported than the administration's 'terminated' claim. The needle rests on those facts — not on either side's sincerity about the Constitution, which the precedent shows is power-contingent on both sides.

pattern.thesis: This vote is one instance of a recurring, decades-long pattern: Congress periodically passes a war powers resolution to rein in a president's unauthorized use of force, the president vetoes or ignores it, and the underlying Article I/Article II question is never resolved by a court. Treating the 2026 vote as a singular constitutional crisis misses that it is the latest entry in a long ledger of symbolic rebukes that have never once forced a withdrawal — which is both the left's indictment of executive overreach and the right's evidence that the rebuke is toothless.

campaigns.where: June 2026 — House Iran war powers resolution (today's issue)
campaigns.what: House passed 215-208 directing removal of U.S. forces from hostilities against Iran absent authorization; Senate advanced a parallel measure 50-47 (May 19) but has not given final passage; Trump signaled a veto.
campaigns.authorization: Passed House; Senate not final; veto expected, no veto-proof majority
campaigns.source: PolitiFact — The war powers resolution on the Iran war: What's next?

corroboration.source: CBS News — Senate advances resolution to limit Trump's Iran war powers

campaigns.what: After Trump's strike killed Iranian Gen. Qassem Soleimani, Sen. Kaine's resolution passed both chambers; Trump vetoed it on May 6, 2020, arguing the measure wrongly 'implies that the president's constitutional authority to use military force is limited to defense... against imminent attack.' The Senate failed to override, 49-44.
campaigns.authorization: Passed both chambers; vetoed; override failed
campaigns.source: The White House (archived) — Presidential Veto Message for S.J. Res. 68

corroboration.source: CNN — Trump vetoes Iran War Powers resolution

campaigns.where: 2011 — Libya air campaign and the unenforced 60-day clock
campaigns.what: Obama exceeded the 60-day War Powers deadline (May 20, 2011) without authorization, arguing Libya operations were not 'hostilities' under the statute; Speaker Boehner objected, but Congress never compelled an end. The OLC's April 1, 2011 opinion held the operations were not 'sufficiently extensive' to be a 'war' requiring prior approval.
campaigns.authorization: Never authorized; clock exceeded; no congressional enforcement
campaigns.source: ProPublica — What Exactly Is the War Powers Act and Is Obama Really Violating It?

pattern.takeaway: The recurring question is whether the War Powers Resolution is a binding constraint or a dead letter that presidents of both parties route around with the same 'this isn't really hostilities' argument. The 'one-off' tell to watch: anyone framing 2026 as a unique constitutional emergency (or a uniquely frivolous stunt) without acknowledging that the identical fight, with the parties reversed, played out under Obama in 2011 and Trump in 2020 — which is the context that turns a partisan shouting match into a real structural question about who gets to start and end wars.

notAdjudicable: Whether the 2026 war on Iran was wise, justified, or worth its human and fiscal cost — a strategic and moral judgment, not a fact the War Powers debate settles.

Whether Congress should reclaim war powers from the executive, or whether modern security requires a strong, fast-acting commander-in-chief — a constitutional-design value question, not an empirical one.
Whether the War Powers Resolution is itself constitutional — a contested legal question every president since 1973 has disputed and no court has definitively resolved.

Whether a ceasefire with ongoing sporadic strikes should count as 'peace' or 'war' for political purposes — partly factual (are strikes occurring?) and partly a definitional/values judgment about when a war has 'ended.'
Whether the four Republicans who crossed party lines are principled constitutionalists or 'grandstanders' — a judgment about motive that the evidence cannot adjudicate.
biasWatch: The dominant distortion here is power-contingent constitutionalism: people's certainty about who controls the use of force flips with which party holds the White House. The tell to watch for in yourself and in commentary: does the speaker treat the 60-day War Powers clock as sacred only when the other party's president blew past it? Run the test on Libya 2011 — if Obama narrowing 'hostilities' to dodge the deadline felt like prudent restraint, but Trump narrowing 'hostilities' via a ceasefire feels like a lawless power grab (or vice versa), the objection is partisan, not principled. The honest, non-partisan questions survive the test: did the clock lapse, was the war authorized, and are hostilities actually ongoing — argue those facts, not the team.
